data-engineer.overview: This system is a Python-centric computer vision stack with a configurable data pipeline. Data flows from user-specified dataset configs (e.g., COCO, VOC) through loaders and augmentation into training and inference, as captured in $.devInsight.architecture.dataFlow. Core dependencies are PyTorch and torchvision, plus ecosystem tools for export and deployment noted in $.devInsight.architecture.depsSummary. Component structure ⚠️, including model architectures, data loaders, and tracking/solutions layers, is summarized in $.devInsight.codeOverview.components. Runtime performance profiles ⚠️ for detector variants on COCO are tracked in $.devInsight.runtimePerf to guide dataset and model selection trade-offs.

data-scientist.overview: The ML system card (docs/ml_system_card.yaml) describes Ultralytics YOLO as an open‑source computer vision framework and model zoo ⚠️ (see $.business.executiveSummary). It documents intended users as developers and researchers applying detection, segmentation, pose, and related vision tasks ⚠️ ($.business.intendedUse, $.business.userPopulations). Out‑of‑scope applications include non‑vision ML and certain general‑purpose NLP uses ⚠️ ($.business.outOfScopeUse). Hazardous uses focus on YOLO not acting as the sole decision‑maker in safety‑critical settings ⚠️ ($.business.hazardousUseCases). Key model KPIs are primarily COCO mAP metrics across YOLO variants ⚠️ ($.business.kpis).
data-scientist.changes: - No significant updates detected in the business-facing portions of the system card for this run relative to the provided baseline; any apparent content in $.business.executiveSummary, $.business.intendedUse, $.business.userPopulations, $.business.outOfScopeUse, $.business.hazardousUseCases, and $.business.kpis should be treated as potentially unchanged ⚠️.

domain-expert.overview: ⚠️ $.business.executiveSummary describes Ultralytics YOLO as an open-source computer vision framework and model zoo used for image- and video-based tasks. ⚠️ According to $.business.intendedUse, it targets developers, researchers, and organizations building detection, segmentation, classification, pose, and tracking systems. ⚠️ $.business.userPopulations distinguishes AGPL-3.0 package users from commercial-license customers deploying in production or at scale. ⚠️ $.business.outOfScopeUse and $.business.hazardousUseCases emphasize that YOLO should not be the sole decision-maker in safety‑critical, surveillance, or legally consequential workflows without human oversight and domain‑specific validation.
domain-expert.changes:
- No significant updates detected to high-level business framing in $.business.* for this run.
- ⚠️ Current content for $.business.hazardousUseCases, $.business.outOfScopeUse, and $.business.userPopulations may differ from the baseline, but the diff could not be reliably established from the available signals.
- Domain experts should treat all business-level descriptions (intended use, KPIs in $.business.kpis, and user segments) as provisional and verify against the rendered ML System Card in docs/ml_system_card.yaml.

governance-compliance-ethics-officer.overview: The ML System Card’s governance section describes how the project approaches testing, licensing, and technical risk management across the codebase. It references governance assessments, policies, and a risk register for the Ultralytics ecosystem, including models, training, export, and deployment components. These entries help Governance, Compliance & Ethics functions understand how reliability, open-source obligations, and safety-related technical risks are being tracked and mitigated over time across multiple backends and integrations. ⚠️
governance-compliance-ethics-officer.changes:
- $.governance.assessments: Expanded description of automated unit and integration test coverage for CLI, training, validation, and related workflows, emphasizing continuous quality checks across critical paths. ⚠️
- $.governance.policies: Clarified licensing and policy language, including reference to OSI-approved GNU Affero General Public License terms for core library and documentation, aligning with open-source governance expectations. ⚠️
- $.governance.riskRegister: Updated technical risk entry highlighting potential export and deployment bugs across diverse inference backends (e.g., ONNX, TensorRT, TFLite), with corresponding mitigation considerations. ⚠️

governance-officer.overview: The ML system’s governance focuses on testing, licensing, and operational risk tracking. ⚠️ Automated quality checks reportedly include unit and integration tests for CLI, training, validation, export, and deployment workflows (see $.governance.assessments). ⚠️ Project artifacts appear to be covered by an OSI‑approved GNU Affero GPL‑family license, with additional terms for models and datasets (see $.governance.policies). ⚠️ A risk register tracks technical issues across multi‑backend export and deployment (see $.governance.riskRegister).
governance-officer.changes: - No significant updates detected to governance structures or practices in $.governance.assessments, $.governance.policies, or $.governance.riskRegister based on the current run vs. baseline.

ml-engineer.overview: The vision stack centers on a configurable training/inference engine defined in $.devInsight.architecture.dataFlow. Users select dataset YAMLs (e.g., COCO variants) and model YAMLs (e.g., YOLOv5–YOLO12, RT-DETR, SAM) which drive a unified pipeline spanning data loading, augmentation, model construction, loss/metrics, and export. Core dependencies are PyTorch and torchvision per $.devInsight.architecture.depsSummary, with optional integrations for ONNX, TensorRT, OpenVINO, and other runtimes. Public access is primarily through a YOLO-style CLI and Python APIs in the ultralytics package ⚠️ (from $.devInsight.architecture.publicApis).
ml-engineer.changes:
- Updated or revalidated dependency assumptions for core training/inference on PyTorch and torchvision, and their relationship to export/inference runtimes, as captured in $.devInsight.architecture.depsSummary.
- Clarified end-to-end data and model configuration flow in $.devInsight.architecture.dataFlow, including how dataset and model YAMLs feed into the unified engine.
- Added or revised documentation of CLI/Python public entrypoints in $.devInsight.architecture.publicApis ⚠️.
- Added or updated benchmark records for detection task det_yolo26_coco_val2017 in $.devInsight.runtimePerf, covering mAP and latency/throughput across yolo26 variants ⚠️.

product-manager.overview: Ultralytics YOLO is an open‑source computer vision framework and model zoo ⚠️ (from $.business.executiveSummary). It is intended for developers, researchers, and organizations building vision applications such as detection, segmentation, pose, and tracking ⚠️ (from $.business.intendedUse). Typical user populations include students, researchers, enthusiasts, and commercial users under different licensing terms ⚠️ (from $.business.userPopulations). Out‑of‑scope uses include general non‑vision ML tasks and other domains not supported by the library’s models or data pipeline ⚠️ (from $.business.outOfScopeUse). Hazardous use cases include deploying YOLO as the sole decision‑maker in safety‑critical systems ⚠️ (from $.business.hazardousUseCases).
product-manager.changes:
- Business positioning and high‑level description of Ultralytics YOLO remain focused on open‑source computer vision tooling ⚠️ (from $.business.executiveSummary).
- Intended use continues to emphasize developer and research workflows for vision tasks, with no clearly detectable scope expansion ⚠️ (from $.business.intendedUse).
- User population segments and examples appear stable, with no clearly identifiable shifts in target users ⚠️ (from $.business.userPopulations).
- KPIs for model performance (e.g., COCO mAP targets) are defined but no baseline deltas are inferable from this run ⚠️ (from $.business.kpis).

No significant updates detected.

project-manager.overview: Ultralytics YOLO is documented as an open‑source computer vision framework and model zoo supporting detection, segmentation, pose, classification, and tracking for a wide range of imaging domains (see $.business.executiveSummary⚠️). It is positioned for developers, researchers, and organizations building custom vision solutions rather than end‑user applications (see $.business.intendedUse⚠️). KPIs focus on benchmark accuracy across representative datasets and model families (see $.business.kpis⚠️). The card also distinguishes supported user groups and clarifies high‑risk and out‑of‑scope deployments (see $.business.userPopulations⚠️, $.business.hazardousUseCases⚠️, $.business.outOfScopeUse⚠️).
project-manager.changes:
- Refined high-level description of the framework and its capabilities in $.business.executiveSummary⚠️.
- Clarified intended users (developers, researchers, organizations) and typical workflows in $.business.intendedUse⚠️.
- Added/updated examples of hazardous deployments (e.g., sole decision-maker in safety‑critical systems) in $.business.hazardousUseCases⚠️.
- Expanded explicit out-of-scope scenarios, especially non-vision or general-purpose uses, in $.business.outOfScopeUse⚠️.
- Introduced or adjusted benchmark-driven KPIs (e.g., COCO mAP targets for YOLO26x) in $.business.kpis⚠️.
- Better segmented primary user populations, including open-source users vs. commercial customers, in $.business.userPopulations⚠️.

software-developer.overview: ⚠️ The business section of the ML System Card describes Ultralytics YOLO as an open-source computer vision framework and model zoo used for tasks like detection, segmentation, pose, and classification (see $.business.executiveSummary). ⚠️ Intended users include developers and researchers integrating YOLO into applications via Python, CLIs, and deployment targets (see $.business.intendedUse, $.business.userPopulations). ⚠️ Business KPIs track benchmark metrics such as COCO mAP for key model variants to monitor performance over time (see $.business.kpis).
software-developer.changes:
- No significant updates detected in business-oriented ML System Card fields relevant to software developers.
- Current draft still emphasizes intended usage patterns and user populations, with hazardous and out-of-scope use cases explicitly called out in $.business.hazardousUseCases and $.business.outOfScopeUse ⚠️.
- KPI tracking for core models remains centered on standard vision benchmarks (e.g., COCO metrics) in $.business.kpis ⚠️.

ux-researcher.overview: Ultralytics YOLO is described ⚠️ as an open-source computer vision framework and model zoo ($.business.executiveSummary), intended ⚠️ for developers, researchers, and organizations building vision-based applications ($.business.intendedUse). Current documentation outlines ⚠️ primary user populations such as students, researchers, enthusiasts, and commercial users under different licenses ($.business.userPopulations). The card also enumerates ⚠️ hazardous use cases like using YOLO as a sole decision-maker in safety-critical domains ($.business.hazardousUseCases) and specifies out-of-scope uses including non-vision ML tasks ($.business.outOfScopeUse). Performance KPIs ⚠️ (e.g., COCO mAP targets for YOLO26x) are listed to benchmark model quality ($.business.kpis).
ux-researcher.changes:
- No significant updates detected.

The referenced sections—executive summary ($.business.executiveSummary), intended use ($.business.intendedUse), hazardous and out-of-scope use cases ($.business.hazardousUseCases, $.business.outOfScopeUse), user populations ($.business.userPopulations), and KPIs ($.business.kpis)—appear unchanged in this run. ⚠️

note.overview: Ultralytics YOLO is an open‑source computer vision framework and model zoo ⚠️ (from $.business.executiveSummary). It is intended for developers, researchers, and organizations building vision applications such as detection, segmentation, pose, and tracking ⚠️ (from $.business.intendedUse). Typical user populations include students, researchers, enthusiasts, and commercial users under different licensing terms ⚠️ (from $.business.userPopulations). Out‑of‑scope uses include general non‑vision ML tasks and other domains not supported by the library’s models or data pipeline ⚠️ (from $.business.outOfScopeUse). Hazardous use cases include deploying YOLO as the sole decision‑maker in safety‑critical systems ⚠️ (from $.business.hazardousUseCases).
note.changes:
- Business positioning and high‑level description of Ultralytics YOLO remain focused on open‑source computer vision tooling ⚠️ (from $.business.executiveSummary).
- Intended use continues to emphasize developer and research workflows for vision tasks, with no clearly detectable scope expansion ⚠️ (from $.business.intendedUse).
- User population segments and examples appear stable, with no clearly identifiable shifts in target users ⚠️ (from $.business.userPopulations).
- KPIs for model performance (e.g., COCO mAP targets) are defined but no baseline deltas are inferable from this run ⚠️ (from $.business.kpis).

No significant updates detected.

note.overview: Ultralytics YOLO is documented as an open‑source computer vision framework and model zoo supporting detection, segmentation, pose, classification, and tracking for a wide range of imaging domains (see $.business.executiveSummary⚠️). It is positioned for developers, researchers, and organizations building custom vision solutions rather than end‑user applications (see $.business.intendedUse⚠️). KPIs focus on benchmark accuracy across representative datasets and model families (see $.business.kpis⚠️). The card also distinguishes supported user groups and clarifies high‑risk and out‑of‑scope deployments (see $.business.userPopulations⚠️, $.business.hazardousUseCases⚠️, $.business.outOfScopeUse⚠️).
note.changes:
- Refined high-level description of the framework and its capabilities in $.business.executiveSummary⚠️.
- Clarified intended users (developers, researchers, organizations) and typical workflows in $.business.intendedUse⚠️.
- Added/updated examples of hazardous deployments (e.g., sole decision-maker in safety‑critical systems) in $.business.hazardousUseCases⚠️.
- Expanded explicit out-of-scope scenarios, especially non-vision or general-purpose uses, in $.business.outOfScopeUse⚠️.
- Introduced or adjusted benchmark-driven KPIs (e.g., COCO mAP targets for YOLO26x) in $.business.kpis⚠️.
- Better segmented primary user populations, including open-source users vs. commercial customers, in $.business.userPopulations⚠️.

note.overview: Ultralytics YOLO is described ⚠️ as an open-source computer vision framework and model zoo ($.business.executiveSummary), intended ⚠️ for developers, researchers, and organizations building vision-based applications ($.business.intendedUse). Current documentation outlines ⚠️ primary user populations such as students, researchers, enthusiasts, and commercial users under different licenses ($.business.userPopulations). The card also enumerates ⚠️ hazardous use cases like using YOLO as a sole decision-maker in safety-critical domains ($.business.hazardousUseCases) and specifies out-of-scope uses including non-vision ML tasks ($.business.outOfScopeUse). Performance KPIs ⚠️ (e.g., COCO mAP targets for YOLO26x) are listed to benchmark model quality ($.business.kpis).
note.changes:
- No significant updates detected.
